1. setup

1. Create a Rust Project

Section titled “1. Create a Rust Project”

Create a new project using Cargo

Cargo downloads your Rust project’s dependencies and compiles your project.

This gives us a clean Rust project.

2. Add Logging Support

Section titled “2. Add Logging Support”

We’ll use two crates:

  • Log — provides structured logging macros (info!, warn!, error!, etc.)
  • Colog — configures logging automatically and adds colored output

Add both crates:

Why logging? As we build the lexer and parser, it’s extremely helpful to print structured debug information. Especially when writing error-prone code- like working with text. Colored logs make it even easier to understand what’s happening.

Add this example:

Than to run:

You should see nicely formatted, colored log output.

3. Add Easier Error Handling with Anyhow

Section titled “3. Add Easier Error Handling with Anyhow”

Next, add anyhow

What is anyhow? Anyhow makes adding great debug messages in code really simple. For example, it allows us to add context to an error and propagate it further. This is possible thanks to the Rust’s result type.

This pattern will be extremely useful once we start parsing and want readable error messages.

Example:

Reading from a File

Section titled “Reading from a File”

Create a Test File

Section titled “Create a Test File”

Create a simple text file anywhere in your project for testing file IO. Later we will use it as an input for our parser.

Reading the File

Section titled “Reading the File”

Caution

Loading whole file at once might cause problems when you work with large files. But modern systems have so much memory that for our purposes this won’t be an issue. If you want you might implement it differently, this should be fairly simple.

Reading a file in rust is as simple as:

This gives one big string. But a vec of characters is easier to work with than raw UTF-8 bytes for our purposes. This is because the lexer will be checking char by char and sometimes peeking 1 char ahead.

Practice Tasks (Recommended)

Section titled “Practice Tasks (Recommended)”

If you’re new to Rust, try these small exercises. They will strengthen your understanding of string manipulation which is crucial for writing a lexer.

Split a File into Vec Where Each Element Is a Line

Section titled “Split a File into Vec Where Each Element Is a Line”

Read 15 Characters at a Time

Section titled “Read 15 Characters at a Time”

Sort 15-Character Segments by Their Total Character Code Sum

Section titled “Sort 15-Character Segments by Their Total Character Code Sum”

Example: HELLO = 72 + 69 + 76 + 76 + 79 = 372

Bugs

Section titled “Bugs”

If you find anything to improve in this project’s code, please create an issue describing it on the GitHub repository for this project. For website-related issues, create an issue here.

Support

Section titled “Support”

All pages on this site are written by a human, and you can access everything for free without ads. If you find this work valuable, please give a star to the GitHub repository for this project.
